description: Structured source-of-truth model for a GreenOps data input maturity assessment tool covering scoring, decision-readiness and recommendation themes.

1.label: Initial / Ad hoc
1.description: Inputs are fragmented, reactive, estimate-heavy and not reliable enough for confident decision-making.

2.label: Managed / Repeatable
2.description: Inputs can be gathered and reused in a repeatable way, but coverage, precision and attribution remain inconsistent.

3.label: Defined / Standardised
3.description: Data sources, methods and ownership are documented and standardised across key areas.

4.label: Quantitatively Managed / Measured
4.description: Data quality, granularity, coverage and confidence are measured and controlled using evidence.

5.label: Optimising
5.description: Inputs are continuously improved, increasingly automated and embedded into operational and strategic decision-making.

decision_support_statuses.label: Reporting only
decision_support_statuses.description: Suitable mainly for broad reporting, awareness and retrospective analysis.

decision_support_statuses.label: Directional decision support
decision_support_statuses.description: Useful for indicative prioritisation or broad decisions, but not strong enough for high-confidence operational action.

decision_support_statuses.label: Decision-grade
decision_support_statuses.description: Strong enough to support controlled operational, financial or sustainability decisions in relevant areas.

decision_support_statuses.label: Optimisation-grade
decision_support_statuses.description: Strong enough to support continuous optimisation, active management and tighter governance loops.

cross_cutting_lenses.label: Granularity
cross_cutting_lenses.description: How specific the data is to an asset, service, workload, rack, site, provider or region.

cross_cutting_lenses.label: Coverage
cross_cutting_lenses.description: How much of the relevant estate is included.

cross_cutting_lenses.label: Attribution
cross_cutting_lenses.description: Whether the data can be tied to owners, services, workloads or demand.

cross_cutting_lenses.label: Temporal resolution
cross_cutting_lenses.description: Annual, quarterly, monthly, daily, hourly or near-real-time conditions.

cross_cutting_lenses.label: Locational resolution
cross_cutting_lenses.description: National, grid region, cloud region, site, hall, rack, host or workload-specific conditions.

cross_cutting_lenses.label: Timeliness
cross_cutting_lenses.description: How quickly data becomes available after the underlying activity occurs.

cross_cutting_lenses.label: Method quality
cross_cutting_lenses.description: Whether the method is measured, modelled, proxy-based or assumed, and how fit-for-purpose it is.

cross_cutting_lenses.label: Assurance / lineage
cross_cutting_lenses.description: Whether the source, method and assumptions are transparent and auditable.

domains.name: Asset inventory and configuration data
domains.definition: Assesses whether the technology estate is known well enough to support GreenOps calculations across cloud, on-premise and colocation environments.
domains.why_it_matters: Weak inventory data undermines embodied emissions matching, allocation, lifecycle decisions, service mapping and assurance.

maturity_levels.1: Asset records are incomplete, inconsistent or fragmented. Key fields such as model, owner, location, environment or lifecycle status are often missing.
maturity_levels.2: Core asset data is captured and reused in a repeatable way, but quality varies by domain and significant manual interpretation is still required.
maturity_levels.3: Asset standards are documented and used consistently enough to support standardised GreenOps calculations across much of the estate.
maturity_levels.4: Data quality, completeness, matchability and coverage are measured. The organisation can quantify how much of the estate is calculation-ready.
maturity_levels.5: Asset data is integrated, actively maintained and continuously improved to support lifecycle, financial and GreenOps decisions with minimal manual effort.

questions.text: How complete is the asset inventory across cloud, on-premise and colocation environments?
options: Measured completeness and active quality control
Highly complete, continuously maintained and integrated

questions.text: How well are key GreenOps fields maintained, such as vendor, model, location, owner, service link, lifecycle state and hosting environment?
options: Consistently maintained and integrated across systems

questions.text: To what extent can assets be linked to services, applications or business owners?

supports: basic portfolio inventory discussion
rough reporting and broad hotspot indication
standardised reporting and better prioritisation
confident analysis, attribution and target setting across most of the estate
does_not_support: continuous optimisation everywhere without remaining data gaps

supports: decision-grade lifecycle, allocation and optimisation decisions

low: link assets to hosting environment and lifecycle stage
mid: measure data quality and match rates
high: integrate inventory into operational, financial and sustainability workflows
continuously improve asset lineage and lifecycle coverage

domains.name: Operational power and energy data
domains.definition: Assesses the quality and granularity of electricity and energy inputs used in GreenOps calculations.
domains.why_it_matters: Energy is a foundational input for operational emissions, efficiency analysis, capacity planning and non-IT load allocation.

maturity_levels.1: Energy is estimated using spend, contract values, floor area or broad averages.
maturity_levels.2: Site- or provider-level energy data is collected and reused, but attribution remains broad and proxy-heavy.
maturity_levels.3: Measured energy data is standardised across key environments, with documented methods for IT and infrastructure overhead treatment.
maturity_levels.4: Energy is measured at meaningful operational levels and coverage, cadence and confidence are actively monitored.
maturity_levels.5: Energy inputs are granular, timely and connected to services, workloads or operational demand, enabling optimisation and control.

questions.text: What is the main basis for operational energy calculations?
options: Host/device/workload-level power or strong near-real-time telemetry

questions.text: What is the best sustained granularity available for energy data?
options: Annual or ad hoc totals only

questions.text: How well can energy data be attributed to services, applications, tenants or business demand?
options: Attributable to major environments or sites
Attributable to platforms/services in key areas

supports: very rough footprint estimation
site/provider-level reporting
broad estate trend analysis
standard operational reporting
site benchmarking
better non-IT allocation
measured performance management and stronger optimisation across key environments
does_not_support: fully continuous optimisation in areas lacking telemetry

supports: decision-grade operational optimisation and workload/service-level energy decisions

low: replace spend proxies with measured energy where possible
high: use timely energy signals for optimisation, scheduling and governance

domains.name: Water data
domains.definition: Assesses the quality and applicability of water consumption inputs used in GreenOps calculations, especially for data centres and AI-relevant cooling environments.
domains.why_it_matters: Water can be material in data centre and AI contexts, particularly where cooling design, geography or water stress matter.

maturity_levels.1: Water is not included or is represented only qualitatively.
maturity_levels.2: Site or provider water totals are available for some environments, usually with broad allocation assumptions.
maturity_levels.3: Water collection methods are defined and used consistently where material, especially for data-centre-related calculations.
maturity_levels.4: Water use is measured over time with stronger distinction between source, cooling context and facility relevance.
maturity_levels.5: Water data is timely, decision-grade and used to inform operational choices, location strategy and environmental trade-off decisions.

questions.text: How available is water consumption data for relevant facilities or providers?
How much contextual detail exists for water data, such as potable vs non-potable, cooling mode or local water stress?

supports: qualitative risk discussion only
high-level reporting where material
structured reporting and basic site comparison
does_not_support: timely optimisation or strong water-risk decisioning

supports: measured water performance management and better trade-off analysis
does_not_support: continuous optimisation everywhere without remaining data gaps

supports: decision-grade site, cooling and sustainability choices involving water

mid: add geographic context such as water stress
high: use water data in siting, cooling and AI hosting decisions

domains.name: Infrastructure efficiency metric quality
domains.definition: Assesses the accuracy and appropriateness of PUE, WUE, CUE, CER, CLF and similar metrics used to allocate non-IT load.
domains.why_it_matters: These metrics often form the bridge between IT activity and total facility impact. Weak metrics distort GreenOps calculations.

maturity_levels.1: PUE, WUE, CUE and related metrics are assumed, stale or based on design or marketing values.
maturity_levels.2: Standard infrastructure metrics are used consistently, but remain broad, infrequently updated and only partly validated.
maturity_levels.3: Methods and boundaries for using infrastructure metrics are documented and standardised, with clear distinction between measured and estimated values.
maturity_levels.4: Infrastructure metrics are operationally measured, reviewed and validated over time, including site and seasonal variation.
maturity_levels.5: Infrastructure metrics are used with clear boundary discipline, known limitations and sufficient granularity to support credible non-IT load allocation and improvement activity.

questions.text: What best describes the basis of PUE/WUE/CUE-style metrics used in calculations?
options: Documented standard values with boundary rules
Decision-grade measured values with clear limitations and boundary discipline

questions.text: How well do these metrics reflect real variation such as seasonality, site differences or operating mode?

supports: very rough overhead allocation only
repeatable high-level overhead allocation
standardised reporting and better allocation discipline
measured overhead allocation and improvement tracking
does_not_support: continuous high-granularity allocation without remaining gaps

supports: decision-grade non-IT load allocation and provider/facility challenge

low: avoid using design values as operational truth
high: apply metrics only at appropriate boundaries and use them to drive improvement

domains.name: Embodied emissions data
domains.definition: Assesses the quality of embodied carbon inputs for hardware, services and products.
domains.why_it_matters: Embodied data is essential for lifecycle, procurement, refresh, reuse and circularity decisions.

maturity_levels.1: Embodied impact is absent or estimated using spend-based factors only.
maturity_levels.2: Category or sub-category averages are used consistently enough for rough portfolio reporting.
maturity_levels.3: Standard matching methods exist for using vendor averages or product family proxies.
maturity_levels.4: Product- or vendor-specific totals from LCAs, EPDs or declared data are used increasingly across the estate.
maturity_levels.5: Product-specific lifecycle breakdowns support refresh, reuse, circularity and supplier challenge decisions.

questions.text: What is the main basis for embodied emissions calculations?
How well can assets or services be matched to the correct embodied factor?
options: High-confidence product-level matching with lifecycle detail

supports: broad directional reporting only
rough hotspot analysis
structured portfolio analysis and better prioritisation
does_not_support: high-confidence supplier or product challenge everywhere

supports: stronger refresh, procurement and supplier decisions across covered areas
does_not_support: full lifecycle optimisation where stage breakdown is absent

supports: decision-grade lifecycle, procurement and circularity optimisation

low: use sub-category or vendor data where possible
mid: distinguish refurbished, redeployed and new assets
high: use lifecycle-stage data to optimise refresh and circularity decisions

domains.name: Carbon intensity and emissions factors
domains.definition: Assesses the quality of factors used to translate energy, activity or service use into carbon impact.
domains.why_it_matters: Weak factor selection can distort otherwise solid calculations, especially where time and location matter.

maturity_levels.1: Emissions rely on broad annual factors with little locational or methodological precision.
maturity_levels.2: Standard factor sets are used consistently, but remain relatively coarse.
maturity_levels.3: Clear rules exist for location-based, market-based and supplier-specific factors, and are applied consistently.
maturity_levels.4: Factor quality is actively managed, with stronger temporal and locational relevance where needed.
maturity_levels.5: Carbon intensity data is sufficiently granular and well-governed to support location- and time-sensitive operational decisions.

questions.text: How granular are the emissions factors used?
options: Defined rules for location vs market-based usage
More granular temporal or locational factors

questions.text: How well governed is the choice and use of factors?

supports: basic emissions estimates
standard operational reporting
reliable structured reporting including market/location distinctions
better operational and strategic decisions using improved factor relevance
does_not_support: continuous optimisation everywhere without remaining granularity gaps

supports: decision-grade time- and location-sensitive carbon choices

low: avoid over-reliance on coarse annual averages
mid: improve locational and temporal relevance where material
high: use factor quality to inform siting, scheduling and optimisation choices

domains.name: Asset, workload, application and service usage maturity
domains.definition: Assesses whether the organisation understands whether infrastructure and services are actually being used efficiently and meaningfully, including AI-relevant service usage patterns.
domains.why_it_matters: GreenOps depends on understanding not just what is running, but whether it is justified, efficient and delivering value relative to demand.

maturity_levels.1: Utilisation and service usage are poorly understood and not managed systematically.
maturity_levels.2: Basic infrastructure or service usage data is available and reviewed periodically.
maturity_levels.3: Utilisation and usage are assessed using agreed methods and thresholds, enabling systematic identification of idle or oversized resources.
maturity_levels.4: Usage is linked increasingly to applications, services, owners or business context and used for rightsizing, consolidation and optimisation decisions.
maturity_levels.5: Utilisation and usage signals are embedded into operational control and governance, enabling continuous challenge of low-value or avoidable demand.

questions.text: How well is infrastructure utilisation understood at host, VM, cluster or platform level?
How well are application or service usage metrics understood, such as transactions, active users, feature use or demand patterns?
options: Linked to ownership and operational decisions
Used systematically to optimise demand and service design

questions.text: Where AI services are relevant, how well are AI-specific usage metrics understood, such as prompts, tokens, inference calls, model routing or training jobs?
options: Tracked for some services or pilots
Measured and reviewed for key AI services
Actively used for AI demand governance and optimisation

supports: basic anecdotal challenge only
broad rightsizing discussions
does_not_support: service-level optimisation or strong low-value demand challenge

supports: structured identification of underused resources and services
does_not_support: consistent optimisation using business context everywhere

supports: decision-grade rightsizing and service optimisation across key areas
does_not_support: continuous optimisation where usage/business signals remain weak

supports: continuous optimisation of infrastructure, services and AI demand based on real usage

low: improve VM and host utilisation visibility
track application and service demand metrics
mid: link usage to service owners and business context
introduce AI-specific usage measures where relevant
high: embed usage signals into rightsizing, decommissioning, service design and AI governance

domains.name: Allocation and attribution logic
domains.definition: Assesses whether GreenOps impact can be credibly assigned to teams, services, workloads, tenants or consumers.
domains.why_it_matters: Without credible attribution, most GreenOps data remains descriptive rather than actionable.

maturity_levels.1: GreenOps outputs are mostly portfolio totals with little actionable attribution.
maturity_levels.2: Simple allocation rules exist and can be repeated, but are still broad proxies.
maturity_levels.3: Allocation methods are documented and standardised, allowing attribution to major business units, environments or service groups.
maturity_levels.4: Attribution quality is measured and improved, with increasing ability to link impact to defined services, workloads or owners.
maturity_levels.5: Allocation becomes more dynamic and usage-driven, supporting action at the level where change can actually be made.

questions.text: What best describes the current basis of allocation?
How actionable is the current attribution model?
options: Actionable for major groups or services
Actionable at the level where change is made

supports: portfolio-level discussion only
basic prioritisation and broad accountability
structured chargeback/showback-style or accountability decisions
stronger operational and ownership decisions across many services
decision-grade attribution aligned to real operational levers

high: use dynamic and usage-informed attribution where it improves decisions

domains.name: Cloud service telemetry and workload data
domains.definition: Assesses the quality of cloud-native usage, runtime, storage, region and service data used in GreenOps analysis.
domains.why_it_matters: Cloud decisions depend on service-level visibility, region awareness, utilisation and architecture-specific usage patterns.

maturity_levels.1: Cloud GreenOps relies mainly on spend or provider dashboard outputs.
maturity_levels.2: Core cloud usage and cost data is captured and reused in repeatable calculations.
maturity_levels.3: Standardised cloud data inputs exist across accounts, subscriptions and service categories.
maturity_levels.4: Telemetry includes stronger use of runtime, storage, region, utilisation and service-level activity signals.
maturity_levels.5: Cloud inputs are embedded into engineering and operational decision-making, including optimisation of architecture, scheduling and workload placement.

questions.text: What best describes the cloud input data available for GreenOps?
options: Strong engineering-grade telemetry used in decisions

questions.text: How well are region, service type and workload behaviour captured?
options: Strongly captured and used in optimisation

supports: rough cloud footprint and trend discussion
standard cloud reporting and broad optimisation
better cloud accountability and prioritisation
does_not_support: consistent workload- or service-level optimisation everywhere

supports: stronger engineering and platform decisions across key services
does_not_support: continuous optimisation where telemetry remains thin

supports: decision-grade cloud engineering and optimisation

low: capture more than spend and provider dashboards
mid: add runtime, storage and service activity context
link cloud telemetry to owners and workloads
high: use telemetry in engineering workflows, scheduling and architecture decisions

domains.name: Colocation and third-party provider data quality
domains.definition: Assesses the quality, transparency and granularity of external provider data used in GreenOps calculations.
domains.why_it_matters: Third-party environments are often the weakest link in enterprise GreenOps because transparency, boundaries and cadence are poor.

maturity_levels.1: Third-party environments are represented mainly by contracts, invoices or generic assumptions.
maturity_levels.2: Some provider-reported figures are collected and reused, but remain incomplete or weakly attributable.
maturity_levels.3: Minimum data expectations are defined and consistently applied to external providers.
maturity_levels.4: Providers supply measurable and attributable data with stronger transparency over methods and coverage.
maturity_levels.5: Third-party data is sufficiently timely, granular and credible to support operational management, benchmarking and provider challenge.

questions.text: What best describes the external provider data available?
options: Defined minimum data requirements and repeatable reporting
Measured attributable data with method transparency

questions.text: How well can providers be challenged or compared using the data available?
options: Meaningful challenge and comparison across key providers

supports: rough inclusion of provider environments only
repeatable broad provider reporting
minimum viable provider governance and structured reporting
does_not_support: fine-grained management where provider transparency remains weak

supports: stronger provider benchmarking and contract/performance challenge
does_not_support: continuous fine-grained optimisation without timely data

supports: decision-grade provider comparison, challenge and portfolio management

low: separate invoice data from operational data
mid: improve cadence, granularity and methodology transparency
high: use provider data actively in benchmarking, governance and contract decisions

domains.name: Temporal granularity and timeliness
domains.definition: Assesses how current and how time-resolved the data is, and whether that is fit for the decisions being made.
domains.why_it_matters: Many GreenOps decisions depend on when data is measured, how quickly it becomes available and whether variation over time is visible.

maturity_levels.1: Data is collected irregularly and retrospectively.
maturity_levels.2: Data refresh is periodic and repeatable, usually monthly or quarterly at best.
maturity_levels.3: Standard refresh cycles exist by domain and are understood across the organisation.
maturity_levels.4: Timeliness and latency are measured, and data is available quickly enough for performance tracking and forecasting.
maturity_levels.5: Data is sufficiently current and time-resolved to support near-operational optimisation and faster intervention.

questions.text: What best describes the temporal resolution of key GreenOps data?
options: Measured timeliness with stronger operational usefulness

questions.text: How quickly is data available after the activity occurs?
options: Timely enough for faster operational decisions

supports: retrospective reporting only
periodic management reporting
predictable reporting and structured performance tracking
forecasting and more responsive management
time-sensitive optimisation and rapid intervention where needed

low: improve cadence where it affects decision quality
mid: align temporal resolution to decision need
high: use higher-resolution data where it unlocks operational value

domains.name: Data lineage, transparency and assurance
domains.definition: Assesses whether GreenOps numbers can be traced, explained and trusted.
domains.why_it_matters: Even detailed data is weak if the source, method and assumptions are opaque or unauditable.

maturity_levels.1: Sources, assumptions and calculations are opaque or poorly documented.
maturity_levels.2: Calculation methods are partly documented and repeatable, but hard to audit.
maturity_levels.3: Sources, methods, assumptions and responsibilities are documented and controlled.
maturity_levels.4: Lineage, confidence and exception handling are measured and reviewed.
maturity_levels.5: GreenOps data is transparent, reproducible and audit-ready, with active improvement of assurance quality.

questions.text: How visible and documented are data sources, assumptions and calculation steps?
How well are exceptions, confidence levels and estimation boundaries handled?

supports: basic internal discussion only
repeatable internal reporting
structured governance and clearer stakeholder trust
does_not_support: high-assurance external scrutiny in all cases

supports: stronger assurance, governance and decision confidence
does_not_support: full audit-readiness everywhere without remaining gaps

supports: high-confidence governance, assurance and external scrutiny

high: prepare for assurance and audit-style scrutiny

domains.name: Decision integration and optimisation workflow
domains.definition: Assesses whether GreenOps data inputs are actually used to drive operational, architectural, financial and strategic decisions.
domains.why_it_matters: The point of GreenOps is not measurement alone. It is better decisions and measurable improvement.

maturity_levels.1: Data is used mainly for reporting or reactive requests.
maturity_levels.2: Data occasionally informs one-off decisions, but not systematically.
maturity_levels.3: GreenOps inputs are embedded in defined governance and review processes.
maturity_levels.4: Decisions and outcomes are tracked, allowing the organisation to measure the effect of interventions.
maturity_levels.5: GreenOps data is part of normal operational and strategic management, supporting continuous optimisation across the estate.

questions.text: How is GreenOps data currently used in decision-making?
options: Embedded in normal management and optimisation

questions.text: How well are the outcomes of GreenOps actions measured and fed back into future decisions?
options: Strong feedback loops and continuous improvement

supports: reporting and awareness raising
some tactical decisions
does_not_support: systematic improvement or strong governance integration

supports: repeatable governance and prioritisation decisions
measured improvement management and better business cases
embedded GreenOps decision-making and continuous improvement

low: embed GreenOps data into governance forums
mid: connect data to architecture, lifecycle and optimisation decisions
high: build strong feedback loops and continuous improvement routines
